metadata.summary: Production-ready GCP architectural patterns for GKE clusters, Cloud SQL, Cloud Storage with CDN, microservices on Cloud Run, and data pipelines (Dataflow + BigQuery).

content.title: GCP Architecture Patterns
content.summary: Production-ready GCP architectural patterns for GKE clusters, Cloud SQL, Cloud Storage with CDN, microservices on Cloud Run, and data pipelines (Dataflow + BigQuery). The concrete rule is: always use regional GKE clusters with private nodes, Workload Identity, and managed Prometheus; always set Cloud SQL `availability_type = REGIONAL` with PITR for production; always use Spot VMs with taints for batch workloads.

why.p: GCP's defaults favor availability over security—public IPs, zonal databases, no Workload Identity. Following these patterns eliminates the most common production failures: single-AZ database outages, secret leaks via node metadata, and cold-start latency from under-sized node pools.

list.item: Deploying production GKE clusters (regional, private, node pool strategy).
Setting up Cloud SQL PostgreSQL with HA, PITR, and read replicas.
Implementing CDN for static assets via Cloud Storage + Global LB.
Building microservices with Cloud Run, Pub/Sub, and Secret Manager.
Designing data pipelines (batch ETL or streaming with Dataflow + BigQuery).
Migrating from AWS to GCP equivalent services.
GCP project hierarchy, IAM, and billing basics—use `gcp-arch-basics`.
GCP Cloud Storage lifecycle and CMEK—use `gcp-storage`.

section.name: gke-cluster-patterns
section.p: Regional cluster rules, node pool strategies, and security configuration for Workload Identity, Binary Authorization, and Shielded Nodes.

rule.statement: Deploy GKE Standard or Autopilot clusters as REGIONAL (not zonal) with a minimum of 3 zones for production; use Autopilot when teams want to avoid node management overhead.
rule.rationale: Zonal clusters are single points of failure—a zone outage takes the cluster control plane offline. Regional clusters survive individual zone failures with no downtime.

rule.statement: Use the REGULAR release channel for production clusters; avoid RAPID channel in production due to less-tested releases.
rule.rationale: REGULAR channel receives tested Kubernetes versions with a minimum 2-week bake period. RAPID receives new versions immediately with less production validation.

rule.statement: Enable Workload Identity on all clusters; use per-namespace Kubernetes Service Accounts bound to GCP service accounts; never use node-level service account keys.
rule.rationale: Workload Identity eliminates node metadata server access for service account keys. It provides per-workload identity rather than per-node, following least privilege.

rule.statement: Enable Binary Authorization with ATTESTOR_BASED policy; require vulnerability scan attestation from Container Analysis before deployment to production.
rule.rationale: Binary Authorization prevents unscanned or unsigned images from running in production, enforcing the supply chain security requirements for SOC2/PCI compliance.

rule.statement: Enable Shielded Nodes with Secure Boot on all node pools; enable Dataplane V2 (eBPF) for network policy enforcement.
rule.rationale: Shielded Nodes verify node integrity against bootkit and rootkit attacks. Dataplane V2 provides granular network policy enforcement at the kernel level.

rule.statement: Remove the default node pool after cluster creation; use dedicated node pools with autoscaling for each workload tier (system, application, batch); add taints to Spot node pools.
rule.rationale: The default node pool has fixed configuration unsuitable for production. Dedicated pools allow per-workload machine type, autoscaling bounds, and preemption policies.

rule.statement: Configure Spot VM node pools with a Spot:OnDemand taint for fault-tolerant batch workloads; use resource quotas per namespace to prevent runaway autoscaling.
rule.rationale: Spot VMs cost 60-91% less than On-Demand but can be preempted with 30-second notice. Taints ensure only workloads designed for preemption land on Spot nodes.

antipattern.description: Single-zone GKE clusters for production workloads.
antipattern.reason: A zone outage takes the entire cluster offline—control plane and all worker nodes. Regional clusters survive zone failures with automatic rescheduling.

section.name: cloud-sql-patterns
section.p: Cloud SQL HA configuration, read replica patterns, AlloyDB vs Spanner decision, and backup rules.

rule.statement: Always set availability_type = REGIONAL for production Cloud SQL instances; enable PITR (point-in-time recovery) with 30-day retention; disable public IP.
rule.rationale: ZONAL instances have no automatic failover—a zone outage requires manual intervention. REGIONAL provides automatic failover to a standby in a different zone within 60 seconds.

rule.statement: Enable Query Insights and set logging flags: log_checkpoints, log_connections, log_disconnections, log_lock_waits for PostgreSQL; enable deletion protection on production instances.
rule.rationale: Query Insights identifies slow queries without performance overhead. Connection and lock logging provides the audit trail required for compliance. Deletion protection prevents accidental drops.

rule.statement: Configure SSL enforcement (require_ssl = true); use Private Service Connect for connections from GKE, never expose Cloud SQL via public IP.
rule.rationale: Public IP Cloud SQL instances are frequently found in security audits. Private connectivity eliminates the attack surface for credential stuffing and brute-force attacks.

rule.statement: Create read replicas in the same region as the primary for read-heavy workloads; create cross-region replicas for disaster recovery when RPO is under 1 hour.
rule.rationale: Same-region replicas add no cross-region data transfer costs and reduce primary load. Cross-region replicas provide an independent copy for DR failover.

rule.statement: Use Cloud SQL PostgreSQL for standard OLTP workloads; use AlloyDB for PostgreSQL-compatible workloads requiring 2x+ query throughput; use Spanner for global multi-region consistency with 99.999% SLA.
rule.rationale: AlloyDB delivers 2x transactional and 100x analytical throughput over Cloud SQL via columnar engine. Spanner provides external consistency across regions at significantly higher cost.

antipattern.description: Cloud SQL with availability_type = ZONAL in production.
antipattern.reason: ZONAL provides no automatic failover. A zone outage requires manual promotion of a replica, causing 5-30 minutes of downtime depending on replication lag.

section.name: cloud-run-patterns
section.p: Cloud Run patterns, Pub/Sub with DLQ, Dataflow streaming, and BigQuery partitioning rules.

rule.statement: Use Cloud Run for stateless HTTP workloads; bind each Cloud Run service to a dedicated service account with minimal IAM roles; set concurrency and CPU allocation based on workload type.
rule.rationale: Per-service accounts enable auditing of which service accessed which resource. Incorrect concurrency leads to cold start storms (too low) or memory exhaustion (too high).

rule.statement: Enable VPC connector or Direct VPC egress for Cloud Run services that need to access Cloud SQL, Memorystore, or internal services; do not expose internal APIs via public Cloud Run URLs.
rule.rationale: VPC egress routes Cloud Run traffic through the VPC, enabling access to private resources without public exposure or NAT gateway costs.

rule.statement: Configure a dead-letter topic on every Pub/Sub subscription with max_delivery_attempts = 5; use exactly-once delivery for financial and idempotency-critical workflows.
rule.rationale: Without a dead-letter topic, messages that fail processing are retried indefinitely, blocking the subscription and delaying subsequent messages. DLT enables failure inspection and replay.

rule.statement: Use Push subscriptions (Cloud Run, Cloud Functions) for event-driven workloads; use Pull subscriptions for batch consumers that need to control processing rate.
rule.rationale: Push subscriptions trigger immediately on message arrival. Pull subscriptions give consumers control over throughput and prevent overloading downstream systems.

section.name: dataflow-bigquery-patterns
section.p: Dataflow and BigQuery patterns for efficient data pipelines and query optimization.

rule.statement: Partition BigQuery tables on ingestion date or a high-cardinality timestamp field; cluster on the most common filter columns; use Reservations for critical queries.
rule.rationale: Partitioning reduces bytes scanned per query by 10-100x. Clustering further reduces data scanned within partitions. On-demand pricing without reservations can create unpredictable costs for large queries.

rule.statement: Design Dataflow pipelines to be idempotent and restartable; use checkpointing for streaming pipelines; define watermarks for late-arriving data handling.
rule.rationale: Dataflow workers can fail and restart mid-job. Non-idempotent pipelines produce duplicate records on restart. Watermarks define when a window is considered complete.

antipattern.description: Pub/Sub subscription without a dead-letter topic for production workloads.
antipattern.reason: A single bad message can block an entire subscription if there is no DLT to absorb it after max retries. The subscription falls arbitrarily behind with no alerting mechanism.

section.name: cdn-and-storage
section.p: Cloud Storage with CDN for static assets and global load balancing patterns.

rule.statement: Use Cloud Storage + Cloud CDN + Global HTTP(S) Load Balancer for static assets and public APIs. Configure caching headers (Cache-Control: public, max-age) to maximize CDN hit ratio. Use Cloud Armor at the load balancer edge for DDoS protection and WAF rules.
rule.rationale: CDN caches assets in 100+ edge locations, reducing latency for global users. Cloud Armor blocks attacks at the edge before they consume origin bandwidth.

checklist.item: GKE cluster is REGIONAL (3+ zones) with private nodes
Workload Identity enabled on all clusters
Shielded Nodes enabled on all pools
Cloud SQL instances are REGIONAL with PITR enabled
Cloud SQL has deletion protection enabled
Database connections use Private Service Connect
Query Insights enabled on production databases
Cloud Run services use VPC egress for private access
BigQuery tables are partitioned and clustered
Cloud CDN configured for static assets
Cloud Armor rules applied at load balancer
Spot VMs configured with taints for batch workloads
